w:document.mc:Ignorable: w14 w15 w16se w16cid w16 w16cex w16sdtdh w16sdtfl w16du wp14

w:r.w:t: t is time to refine your design by incorporating appropriate design patterns. Your task is to identify
within your system where a design pattern can improve flexibility, maintainability, or scalability. For each situation, you must:
Justify the choice of the design pattern and explain how it addresses the issue.
or sequence diagrams) to illustrate how the pattern improves the design.
Discuss the potential consequences of not using the pattern—how would the design be affected without it?
This step will help you transition from analysis to a more structured and reusable software design, ensuring that your system is well-architected and adaptable for future enhancements.
If your system calculates rental fees and pricing rules vary (e.g., category of vehicles, standard, weekend, …), the
can be used to encapsulate different pricing algorithms. The rental component delegates the calculation to the selected strategy, which supports
, since new pricing rules can be added without modifying existing logic. Other patterns, such as
This can be a possible explanation in your document.
Now that you have identified design problems and applied suitable design patterns to improve the architecture of your system, your next task is to update your class diagram to reflect these changes.
Create a new class diagram that incorporates the changes introduced by the selected design patterns. Ensure it includes key classes, relationships, and relevant methods.
Add a brief description explaining the key differences and improvements between the original domain model
and updated diagram. Your description should highlight:
The patterns applied to solve them.
How these changes improve flexibility, maintainability, and scalability.
This step will help you visualize how design patterns influence the system structure and reinforce the importance of good software design practices.
For Sprint 3, you are expected to demonstrate concrete implementation progress based on your architecture, use cases, and sequence diagrams from Phase II.
must be fully implemented and demonstrated:
Any provider-level operations defined in your use cases
(These can remain abstracted services, but navigation must work.)
At least one working rental-related analytic
Your implementation must reflect your selected
in terms of separation of concerns and components services and responsibilities)
do not need to be fully completed in Sprint 3; however, partial implementation is encouraged.
Full implementation of additional features will be required in
This sprint focuses on demonstrating that your system is operational and aligned with your design decisions.
The whole assignment is submitted by the due date under the corresponding assignment link. It
be completed by ALL members of the team and only one file in PDF or WORD format will be submitted.
Clearly include the names and student IDs of all members of the team in the submission. Indicate the team leader.

One copy of the assignment is to be submitted. You must make sure that you upload the project to the correct link on Moodle. No email submissions are accepted. Projects uploaded to the wrong system, wrong folder, or submitted via email will be discarded and no resubmission will be allowed. Make sure you can access Moodle prior to the submission deadline.
The deadline will not be extended.
Naming convention for uploaded file: Create a PDF or WORD file using the following naming convention. The file should be called a#_
, where # is the number of the project phase, and

is the student id of the team leader. For example, for the first project phase, student ID is 12345678 would submit a PDF file named a1_12345678.pdf. Submit your project electronically on Moodle based on the instruction given by your instructor as indicated above:
The bellow shows the submission template for the project.
Project name, Team Name, Team members, student IDs, document purpose (Phase 3: GOF design pattern and class diagram), Date.
The body of the report will be organized as follow.
For at least four design situations:
Consequences if the pattern were not applied.
Complete class diagram including attributes and methods.
Explanation of improvements over previous version.

Implementation will NOT be submitted as part of this document. The implementation will be evaluated during the lab session. All team members are required to attend and actively navigate the project repository with the TA. Each member must be able to explain the architecture, demonstrate implemented features, and justify design decisions.
Insightful, strategic, and highly professional overview showing deep system understanding.
Strong justification, well-integrated UML and clear architectural impact.
Sophisticated pattern integration demonstrating mastery, alternative evaluation, and architectural foresight.
Clean, structured, accurate UML reflecting applied patterns.
Highly precise, professional-grade UML with exceptional clarity and structural consistency.
Partial implementation; weak explanation during demo.
Most required features functional; acceptable explanation.
All core features functional; clear architectural alignment demonstrated.
Robust, stable system; excellent repository navigation; all members demonstrate mastery and architectural reasoning.
Clear structure, consistent terminology, professional writing.
Exceptional technical writing, precision, academic maturity, and presentation quality.
